paragraphs.body: You have done some really cool stuff in your life. You have travelled to over 20 countries and moved your family from Sudan to the UK. You spent a lot of your life working and never complained about it. I have some questions that I really want to know the answers to.

How did you know when to trust a person? How did you stay so hopeful in this hard journey? How did you keep belief in yourself despite all your trials and tribulations? How did you always stay so transparent with me and my siblings? How did you acquire the knowledge to be accountable for any of your actions? How did you learn to keep an amazing work ethic? How did you become comfortable with taking massive risks? How did you teach us to be fearful of failure? How did you build your resilience? How did you gain an understanding that family is everything?

annotations.text: How did you become comfortable with taking massive risks? How did you teach us to be fearful of failure? How did you build your resilience? How did you gain an understanding that family is everything?
annotations.comment: It is clear in questions like these how much you respect and value your dad.

paragraphs.body: Do you remember any times from your childhood? Do you miss the feeling of the sunshining beaming down onto your neck? What was your dad like when you were younger? Was he still as funny as he is now? Was his laugh the same? What were your siblings like when you were younger? How did you act like when you were the big brother? Were you an enforcer or a comedian? Which one of your siblings was the funniest? Which one of your kids did you resemble the most as a child? Do you think about your mums cooking? What was your favourite dish? What was your favourite song? What would you do on the weekends? Did you go out a lot or were you more of an introvert? Were you as good as soccer as you say you are? Do you remember meeting my mom for the first time? What was the wedding like? Where did you go on your honeymoon?

annotations.text: Which one of your kids did you resemble the most as a child? Do you think about your mums cooking? What was your favourite dish? What was your favourite song?
annotations.comment: Here's an example of a place where engaging your father more would help to strengthen the piece. Is there a dish that you associate with your dad or with his mom? Is there a song that reminds you of him? If you tell him what you believe about him as you ask a question, his answers will do a lot to clarify your understanding.

paragraphs.body: When did you get the initial thought to move away from where you spent your childhood? Was it easy to move to the UK? What was going through your mind when you picked the UK? Why did you not want to move to the US? What challenges did you face when you first moved? How hard was it to move and leave all your friends and family in Sudan? What were you excited about when you moved over? Why did you then move from London to Bristol? Did you want to live in a more suburban city? How did you feel starting up a Sudanese community in Bristol by being one of the first? What did you do for fun on the weekends? If you could have moved anywhere else, where would you have moved? What were your future ideas when you moved?

Do you remember when I was first born? What were some of your emotions when I was born? Did you plan Sara’s birthday to be on the same day as mine? What was the first language I spoke? Why did you want us speaking a lot of Arabic in the house? Did you ever plan what you wanted me and my siblings to do before we were born? Did you let us make mistakes to learn from them? You have always motivated me to push myself but what can I do if I am at dead end with nowhere to go? Do you believe that tough love works? How did you teach us that family is the one thing I can always count on?

Why were you and mom very uneasy about me playing rugby? Were you worried about how physical the game was? Why did you let me play football with no hesitation? Did Ezzat’s success play a role in letting me start? Did you always believe that I would be in the position I currently am in? Are you excited about Mo playing? Were you excited about my first game? How did you feel when I got my first offer? Were you concerned when I prioritised football over my A-level exam? I always had the idea of bringing you and Mo on any official visits, did you enjoy the Pitt visit? What are you excited to see in me and my siblings future? Are you excited about my college football debut? If I live in the US in the future, would you and mom want to move here with me? Do you ever think about moving back to Sudan?
I know that there are a lot of questions that I want answered. I know you won't be able to reply in one letter but please get back to me when you get the chance to.
Hope to hear from you soon Love you Pops Ak list from writing tutorial.10
allowing me to play football //his and my future 14

annotations.comment: Hello, This letter format is a very interesting way of getting to know the importance of the relationship between you and your dad. The central focusing idea of this piece is clearly that you value the person that your father is and that you admire him and want to learn from his experiences. The greatest strength of this piece is the clarity of importance that you find in this relationship, and you are able to express that importance through the many questions that you would like him to answer. The words that stand out the most to me throughout this writing are the words of relationship—pops, of course, but also his dad, your siblings and mom, as well as some of their proper names. You are doing a great job here of showing the importance of your entire family while focusing on the unifying force of your father. I can feel the strength of all these connections in every question, largely because you are focusing on positive lessons that your father can teach you about himself, about your family members, and, of course, about you. Obviously, the audience of this piece is your father, most specifically, but, by extension, the audience is your entire family, and with some work, perhaps, even anybody affected by the Sudanese Diaspora. When people choose to immigrate from one country/society to another, they always have their own individual experiences, but they also share a lot of values and cultural experiences with others who have made similar moves. While your family is very specific, I can imagine someone your age whose family moved from Sudan to, say, Minnesota or South Africa might have very similar questions for their own parents. At the present moment, clearly, the purpose of this writing is to elicit responses from your father, but I believe with some additional work, the purpose could be far more wide reaching and culturally significant. I appreciate the focus you’ve put into each question you’ve asked, but I would love to see you focus on fewer questions while including your own thoughts on some of them. For instance, from the very beginning—“How did you know when to trust a person?”—can you think of someone who had a particular importance in your father’s journey, someone who helped him find himself in a new land? Could you tell a quick story in a sentence or two about what you know of that person? This might help you transition into the next question about the journey itself, and this could build a narrative that would have a greater purpose and reach a broader audience. One thing that really registered with me in reading this piece is the notion that our lineage really guides and shapes us in ways that we will never fully understand. I have learned in reading these questions to your father what it is you value and want to know about yourself. After all, like so many philosophers acknowledge: “Every question possesses a power that does not lie in the answer.” That seems particularly true in your writing. Thanks for This,
